serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Kway Teow Goreng Ikan Bilis as typically served at a Malaysian or Singaporean hawker stall weighs approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Char Kway Teow / Fried Kway Teow — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists 'Char Kway Teow' (fried flat rice noodles) as the closest approved match. The ikan bilis (dried anchovy) variant adds modest additional protein and sodium compared to the standard char kway teow entry. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo char kway teow per-100 g data with minor upward adjustment to protein (+0.5 g) and sodium (+50 mg) to reflect the ikan bilis component, consistent with IMR Malaysia MyFCD anchovy-noodle dish data. Confidence is medium due to adaptation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kway Teow Goreng Ikan Bilis. GI of 56 is estimated from the closest analogous food: stir-fried rice noodles / char kway teow, which appears in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 Atkinson et al. international GI tables with a GI of approximately 56 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 71.75) / 100 ≈ 40.2, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 20.5 × 3.5 = 71.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1820 mg per standard 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Kway Teow Goreng Ikan Bilis due to its very high sodium load from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and dried anchovies, which can significantly exceed the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 56) and high glycemic load per serving (~40) from refined rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Kway Teow Goreng Ikan Bilis in small portions and pair it with non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response, as the refined rice noodle base contributes a substantial glycemic load per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content with saturated fat from cooking oil and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size of Kway Teow Goreng Ikan Bilis given its combined burden of high sodium (~1820 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~4.9 g per serving), both of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kway Teow Goreng Ikan Bilis is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean hawker dish consisting of flat rice noodles (kway teow) stir-fried at high heat in a wok with dried anchovies (ikan bilis), egg, bean sprouts, garlic, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and chilli. The dish is a variant of the classic char kway teow, distinguished by the use of crispy dried anchovies as the primary protein and flavour component instead of prawns or Chinese sausage. The noodles are broad, silky, and slightly chewy, absorbing the smoky wok hei (breath of the wok) flavour that is characteristic of well-executed stir-fried noodle dishes. The ikan bilis contribute a salty, umami-rich depth and a crunchy textural contrast. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 162 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 6.2 g and a carbohydrate content of 20.5 g per 100 g, predominantly from the refined rice noodles. The sodium content is notably high at approximately 520 mg per 100 g, making a standard 350 g plate contribute around 1820 mg of sodium — close to the recommended daily limit. The glycemic index is estimated at 56 (medium), and the glycemic load per serving is high at approximately 40, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish contains egg and fish allergens and is generally considered halal. It is a staple at Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops (kopitiams), commonly eaten as a lunch or dinner main course.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD entry exists specifically for Kway Teow Goreng Ikan Bilis. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Foodo 'Char Kway Teow' entry with minor adjustments for the ikan bilis component cross-referenced against IMR Malaysia MyFCD anchovy-noodle dish data. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo 'Char Kway Teow' per-100 g data with upward adjustment to protein (+0.5 g) and sodium (+50 mg) to reflect the ikan bilis (dried anchovy) component; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD source databases for this dish category. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD source databases for this dish category. 4. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on stir-fried rice noodles / char kway teow from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for this specific variant was found. 5. The dish is classified as Malaysian cuisine but is equally prevalent in Singapore hawker culture; multilingual names reflect both contexts. 6. Halal status is typical for ikan bilis variants but may vary by restaurant; consumers should verify with the specific vendor.
